text: The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to meticulously dissect and remove numerous adhesions, ensuring that surrounding structures are not damaged during the process.
The surgeon performs teasing, pulling, and pushing maneuvers to carefully remove adhesions from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully navigates through the surgical field, avoiding unnecessary damage to surrounding tissues while attempting to reach the gallbladder amidst adhesions.
The surgeon encounters very thick adhesions in the surgical field, requiring careful manipulation to avoid damage to surrounding structures.
The gallbladder is visibly adherent to the colon, omentum, and liver, with dense fibrous tissue connecting these structures.
Pus is visibly oozing from the perforated side of the gallbladder, revealing the cavity filled with pus.
The surgeon clears pus and purulent material from the surgical field, addressing the challenges posed by a difficult gallbladder with thick adhesions and inflammation of the gallbladder wall.

The surgeon attempts to identify the liver bed, noting the presence of thin connective tissue between the gallbladder bed and the liver's Glisson capsule. Due to chronic inflammation, the connective tissue is difficult to discern.
The surgeon identifies the tissue layers using a fundus-first approach due to difficulty accessing Calot's triangle, carefully dissecting from the gallbladder fundus towards the liver bed.
The surgeon completes the demarcation and delineation of the gallbladder fundus, allowing for its separation from surrounding tissues, facilitating access to the Calot's triangle.
The surgeon attempts to visualize the neck of the gallbladder, which is located opposite the basilar sulcus, in order to better identify the cystic duct amidst challenging anatomical conditions.
The surgeon carefully separates adhesions around the cystic duct, which appears larger in diameter than usual, along with the cystic artery.

The surgeon reaches the calyx and begins to separate and identify the cystic duct, carefully examining posterior relations to avoid damaging surrounding structures. The tissue is manipulated to prevent angulation during the dissection.

In cases of chronic inflammation, the surgeon encounters accessory blood vessels due to long-standing disease. If anatomical identification is challenging, an additional port is placed in the left mid-clavicle line to improve access and visualization.
The surgeon applies traction to the cystic duct, clamps it, and uses a double V stapler to securely staple the cystic duct stump.
The surgeon staples the cystic artery, ensuring double stapling due to its unusual diameter for secure closure.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to carefully excise the cystic duct, observing the pulsations of the duct amidst inflamed tissue.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to separate the remaining portion of the gallbladder from the liver bed, following a fundus-first approach. The gallbladder is mostly detached, and the surgeon checks the staples applied to the cystic duct and artery for security.
The surgeon identifies a portion of the gallbladder that is slightly intrahepatic and attempts to partially separate it from the lower back of the liver bed using surgical instruments.

During the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to the tissue, ensuring the traction is at a ninety-degree angle from the area of separation to facilitate effective dissection.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed, having previously separated and stapled the cystic duct and artery.
